primary_diagnosis.name: Tinea pedis (interdigital and moccasin-type), acutely exacerbated with maceration and painful fissuring; no secondary bacterial infection

differential.evidence_basis: Intensely itchy, macerated, peeling, malodorous skin strictly in the interdigital web spaces in an adult with communal-shower exposure is the classic picture.
Can itch and scale, but tends to affect the dorsum/contact surfaces rather than the moist interdigital web spaces; the communal-shower history and web-space maceration point away from it.
Fissures are a portal of entry for bacteria; must be actively screened for (spreading erythema, warmth, fever, ascending streaks, purulent discharge) before it can be excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Opening picture is strongly dermatophyte. The safety task is to actively exclude secondary bacterial infection, not to escalate reflexively.

differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile, no spreading erythema, no ascending streaks, no systemic upset, not diabetic — confirms uncomplicated tinea with fissuring rather than cellulitis.
No spreading non-demarcated erythema, no fever, no ascending lymphangitis, pulses intact — cellulitis is screened negative at this encounter (remains the key safety-net trigger).
Can affect the web spaces but is typically smooth reddish-brown rather than scaly and macerated, and shows coral-red Wood's-lamp fluorescence; scaling/maceration favour dermatophyte.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the red-flag screen is negative, the correct destination is topical self-care with safety-netting, not urgent in-person assessment.

differential.diagnosis: tinea pedis, exacerbated, with fissuring — uncomplicated (no secondary infection)
differential.evidence_basis: Classic interdigital + moccasin dermatophytosis with maceration; secondary bacterial infection actively excluded; responds to topical antifungal plus moisture control.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A KOH prep would confirm dermatophyte hyphae but is not required — the clinical picture is diagnostic and treatment is not delayed for it.

red_flags.description: Signs of secondary bacterial cellulitis or systemic infection — spreading non-demarcated erythema up the foot/leg, warmth, fever, ascending red streaks, or an infected diabetic foot
red_flags.clinical_significance: The one genuinely dangerous trajectory of tinea pedis: fissures allow bacterial entry and can progress to cellulitis. At this encounter it is screened negative, so it is the safety-net trigger rather than a present problem.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An evolving cellulitis mistaken for 'just athlete's foot' could progress to deep soft-tissue infection; conversely, over-calling it drives unnecessary urgent referral and antibiotics.

red_flags.description: Deep painful interdigital fissures with serous oozing — a portal of entry for bacterial superinfection
red_flags.clinical_significance: The fissures themselves are not an emergency but define why moisture control and prompt antifungal/antiseptic treatment matter, and why a bacterial-infection safety-net is mandatory.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated macerated fissures can become secondarily infected, converting a benign complaint into cellulitis.

pathophysiology_summary: Tinea pedis is a superficial dermatophyte infection (usually Trichophyton rubrum or T. interdigitale) of the plantar and interdigital skin. Dermatophytes digest keratin and thrive in the warm, dark, humid microenvironment of occlusive footwear and macerated web spaces — hence the association with communal showers and sweaty shoes. In the interdigital pattern, chronic moisture produces white sodden maceration, scaling and painful fissuring; the moccasin pattern produces diffuse hyperkeratotic scaling of the soles and lateral borders. Odour reflects associated bacterial overgrowth of the macerated skin. The principal complication is bacterial superinfection through fissures, which can progress to cellulitis, particularly in people with diabetes or peripheral vascular disease. Treatment is a topical allylamine or azole antifungal plus aggressive moisture control; oral antifungals and antibiotics are reserved for extensive/refractory disease or established bacterial infection respectively.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reading 'weeping, raw, painful' as an emergency and escalating to urgent in-person review or ED when the picture is uncomplicated tinea with fissuring — the source note's own framing (urgent after-hours home visit) is the trap.

Prescribing systemic antibiotics for macerated, malodorous, oozing web spaces in the ABSENCE of cellulitis signs — odour and serous ooze are features of tinea/maceration, not proof of bacterial infection.
Reaching for a topical corticosteroid to calm the itch and inflammation without an antifungal — steroids suppress the local response and let the dermatophyte spread, producing tinea incognito.
Endorsing the patient's habit of packing talcum powder into open cracks — it clumps, retains moisture and irritates; drying antiseptic soaks are the correct moisture-control step.
Correctly treating the tinea but failing to safety-net the one dangerous trajectory (secondary bacterial cellulitis) — the fissures are the reason that safety-net is mandatory.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of pedal pulses and assessment of capillary refill / peripheral perfusion
Whether there is subtle spreading warmth or induration on palpation of the foot
A KOH microscopy preparation for branching dermatophyte hyphae
Formal sensory testing for a diabetic neuropathic foot
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: None urgent. Manage remotely with a topical antifungal plus moisture-control advice and a clear bacterial-infection safety-net; recommend routine GP or pharmacist follow-up if not improving. Escalate only if the patient reports spreading redness, warmth, fever, purulent discharge, or is later found to be diabetic/immunocompromised.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low — the interdigital + moccasin dermatophyte picture is clinically characteristic and telehealth-diagnosable. The real risk is direction of error: over-triage/over-treatment of a benign condition, or missing an emerging secondary bacterial infection.
